revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:藤森神社]][[zh:藤森神社]] {{infobox religious building |名称={{ill|Fujinomori Shrine|ja|藤森神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinomori Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=
拝殿 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|location={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}{{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}{{ill|Fukakusa, Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|ja|深草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa, Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|1=Fukakusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30933141}}{{ill|Toriizakichō|ja|鳥居崎町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriizakichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}609 | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 57 | 緯度秒 = 4.1 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 46 | 経度秒 = 18.1 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |地図 = Japan Kyoto city | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|deity = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi|ja|賀茂別雷命|1=Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami|2=ja|3=賀茂別雷命|4=zh|5=賀茂別雷命|6=simple|7=Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|lt=Wakeikazuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Wakeikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11634943}}, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Nippon Take|1=Yamato Takeru}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|qid=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}}, {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} and others | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|type = Former {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} |創建 = Traditionally in the 3rd year of Empress Jingū’s regency (203 CE) |本殿 = {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=Kirizuma-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13308575}} roofed with {{ill|Hinoki bark|ja|檜皮葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinoki bark|1=cypress bark roof|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q90373477}} |別名 = |札所等 = {{ill|Zenkoku Seiseikai|ja|全国清々会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkoku Seiseikai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (59 shrines venerating Susanoo-no-Mikoto) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival = {{ill|May 5|en|May 5|qid=Q2550|lt=May 5|1=May 5}} (Fujimori Matsuri) |神事 = The Kakeuma (Horseback Acrobatic) Ritual (May 5) |image = Fujimori-jinja_Main_Sanctuary_003.jpg }} {{osm box|w|368465614}} '''Fujinomori Shrine''' (藤森神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. Formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}, it serves as the tutelary shrine of the Fukakusa district in Fushimi-ku and the Honmachi/Fukuhata district in Higashiyama-ku. The shrine is famous for its annual horseback acrobatic event on {{ill|May 5|en|May 5|qid=Q2550|lt=May 5|1=May 5}} (known as the Kakeuma Shinji) and is said to be the place of origin for the {{ill|Iris|ja|菖蒲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iris|1=Q11619948|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619948}} Festival (Shōbu no Sekku). From June to July, its hydrangea garden is open to the public, showcasing around 3,500 hydrangea plants. == Enshrined Deities == * Main Sanctuary (central section): ''' {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, {{ill|Kamo Wakeikazuchi|ja|賀茂別雷命|1=Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami|2=ja|3=賀茂別雷命|4=zh|5=賀茂別雷命|6=simple|7=Kamo Wake-ikazuchi|lt=Wakeikazuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Wakeikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11634943}}, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Nippon Take|1=Yamato Takeru}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}},''' {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|qid=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}}, {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} * East Sanctuary (main sanctuary’s east section): ** {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q335181}}, Emperor Sudō Jinkei ({{ill|Prince Toneri|en|Prince Toneri|qid=Q1362200|lt=Prince Toneri|1=Prince Toneri}}) * West Sanctuary (main sanctuary’s west section): ** Emperor Sudō ({{ill|Prince Sawara|en|Prince Sawara|qid=Q1079634|lt=Prince Sawara|1=Prince Sawara}}), {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予親王|1=Iyo Shinnō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iyo|13=User:Immanuelle/Iyo Shinnō|qq=}}, {{ill|Princess Inoe|en|Princess Inoe|qid=Q7244649|lt=Princess Inoe|1=Princess Inoe}} The central sanctuary deifies gods linked to the {{ill|Sankan Seibatsu|ja|三韓征伐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankan Seibatsu|1=Three Han Campaign|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11249435}} by Empress Jingū. The east sanctuary honors local tutelary gods of the area, and the west sanctuary venerates spirits of revered or potentially vengeful figures.「京都の神社と祭り」p132 == History == [[File:Fujimori-jinja West Torii Gate 001.jpg|thumb|西門参道]]There are various theories about the shrine’s founding date and enshrined deities. According to shrine tradition, Empress Jingū, upon returning victorious from her 3rd-century expedition to the Three Kingdoms of Korea (Sankan Seibatsu), erected a war banner (纛旗) at Fujinomori in the settlement of Fukakusa in Yamashiro Province and buried military equipment to invoke divine protection in the 3rd year of her regency (203 CE). The seven kami of the central sanctuary are associated with this event. A “Banner Mound” (Hata-zuka) stands to the east of the main sanctuary, said to mark where the standard was raised.[[File:Fujimori-jinja Worship Hall 001.jpg|thumb|拝殿]]The present main sanctuary was originally the {{ill|Kashiko-dokoro|ja|賢所|4=fr|5=Kashiko-dokoro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashiko-dokoro}} (the Imperial Household’s inner shrine) granted in Shōtoku 2 (1712) by {{ill|Emperor Nakamikado|en|Emperor Nakamikado|qid=Q314817|lt=Emperor Nakamikado|1=Emperor Nakamikado}}, and it is said to be the oldest existing example of that style. It consists of three sections—east, central, and west.[[山上伊豆母]]『古代神道の本質』 法政大学出版局 1989th year 、ISBN 4588306014 pp.234-239. The middle section of the main sanctuary enshrines seven deities linked to Empress Jingū’s Sankan Seibatsu. The east sanctuary was originally established in Tenpyō-Hōji 3 (759) at Fujio (in present-day Fukakusa Yabunouchi-chō, at the foot of {{ill|Mount Inari (Kyoto)|ja|稲荷山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Inari (Kyoto)|1=Q11596860|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11596860}}) to worship Emperor Sudō Jinkei (Prince Toneri) under the name “Fujio-sha.” In Eikyō 10 (1438), by imperial order of {{ill|Emperor Go-Hanazono|en|Emperor Go-Hanazono|qid=Q451926|lt=Emperor Go-Hanazono|1=Emperor Go-Hanazono}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshinori|en|Ashikaga Yoshinori|qid=Q239170|lt=Ashikaga Yoshinori|1=Ashikaga Yoshinori}}, the 6th Shōgun of the Muromachi shogunate, moved the enshrined deity of Mt. Inari’s summit shrine (enshrined there as Inari Okami) down to the base at Fujio—becoming what is now {{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|en|Fushimi Inari-taisha|qid=Q714828|lt=Fushimi Inari Taisha|1=Fushimi Inari-taisha}}. The Fujio-sha at that site was then relocated to Fujinomori as the east section of the main sanctuary. Because of that, local residents around Fushimi Inari Taisha are still considered ujiko (shrine parishioners) of Fujinomori Shrine. Meanwhile, the old Manabazu (真幡寸) Shrine originally at Fujinomori was moved to its current location (now {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōnan-gū}}). The west sanctuary was established in Enryaku 19 (800) at Tsukamoto (near present-day Higashiyama-ku Honmachi 16-chōme, in the vicinity of {{ill|Tōfuku-ji|en|Tōfuku-ji|qid=Q2166743|lt=Tōfuku-ji|1=Tōfuku-ji}}) to worship Prince Sawara. In Bunmei 2 (1470), it was merged with Fujinomori Shrine. Consequently, residents near Tōfuku-ji remain the shrine’s ujiko. Prince Sawara had revered Fujinomori Shrine during his lifetime. When a rebellion broke out in Mutsu Province, he prayed at this shrine before departing as the commanding general on May 5, which is the origin of the modern Kakeuma (Horseback Acrobatic) Ritual. Additionally, after Prince Sawara was implicated in the 785 assassination of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|en|Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|qid=Q3090668|lt=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu|1=Fujiwara no Tanetsugu}}, he was deposed as Crown Prince and starved himself to death. Enshrining him here gave rise to the name “Fujinomori Tenno-sha” and introduced aspects of Goryō Shinkō (the veneration of vengeful spirits).角川日本地名大辞典 巻26 京都府上巻 p.1241{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}『京都・山城寺院神社大辞典』p587 According to Yoshida Kanetomo’s “Fujimori-sha Engi,” or Volume 5 of the “[[Shūi Miyako Meisho Zue|拾遺都名所図会]],”[https://www.city.kyoto.jp/somu/rekishi/fm/ishibumi/html/hu147.html 京都市Webサイト : 蒙古塚] in Ten’ō 1 (781) under Emperor Kōnin, invading forces from “Mōko” (Mongols) attacked Japan. Prince Sawara led the imperial forces, driving them away, after praying at Fujinomori Shrine. Hence one epithet of the shrine became “Yumi-hyō no Mandokoro” (弓兵政所), and the “Mōko-zuka” (Mongol Mound) on the shrine grounds is said to contain the head of the Mongol general. On the rear left and right of the main sanctuary stand smaller shrines to Hachiman and Daishōgun, respectively, which likely stood in one row alongside the main sanctuary in Eikyō 10 (1438). Beside the unusual “divided worship hall” (割拝殿), which is open down the middle so visitors can pass through, stands a monument inscribed with a poem by {{ill|Kojijū|en|Kojijū|qid=Q3198348|lt=Matsuyoi no Koshozō|1=Kojijū}}: “I saw from a distance what seemed like a purple cloud; it was in fact a stand of tall wisteria in the grove.” This suggests that there was once a large wisteria forest here. The stone walls flanking the south and west entrances were donated during the dismantling of {{ill|Fushimi Castle|en|Fushimi Castle|qid=Q1196273|lt=Fushimi Castle|1=Fushimi Castle}}; carved marks belonging to various feudal lords who contributed to the castle’s construction can still be seen on them. == Distinctive Features == Due to its associations with cavalry rituals (kakeuma), the “Shōbu” (菖蒲, which also means “battle/samurai spirit”) festival, and the fact that numerous war-related deities are enshrined here—plus the use of the surrounding area for military purposes from the Meiji era until the end of WWII—Fujinomori Shrine has long been revered as a shrine for horses and martial victory. Today, it is also known for victory in horse racing, with many horse-racing professionals and fans dedicating votive plaques (ema) showing racehorses. Moreover, because Prince Toneri is enshrined here, many believe it grants academic success and “winning” in exams. The shrine’s forest merges with the wooded area of {{ill|Kyoto University of Education|en|Kyoto University of Education|qid=Q1398915|lt=Kyoto University of Education|1=Kyoto University of Education}} to the east, forming a contiguous grove. The parishioners (ujiko) of Fujinomori Shrine inhabit a broad area extending from near the shrine grounds (Miyamoto Shimo-no-Gō), along the east side of the {{ill|Kamo River|en|Kamo River|qid=Q27218|lt=Kamo River (Kyoto)|1=Kamo River}} up toward Fushimi Inari Shrine (Fukakusa-no-Gō) and the Tōfuku-ji area (Tōfukuji Kami-no-Gō), reaching nearly to the JR Biwako Line.「京都の神社と祭り」p4 Consequently, many who live near Fushimi Inari Taisha are still technically parishioners of Fujinomori Shrine rather than the original Inari shrine, supporting the narrative that “Fujinomori Shrine predated Fushimi Inari in this location.”「京都の神社と祭り」p130-134 This also explains why the Fujinomori mikoshi visits Fushimi Inari Taisha’s grounds during its festival. == Grounds == [[File:Fujimori-jinja Auxiliary Daishogun Shrine 001.jpg|thumb|The Daishōgun-sha building (Important Cultural Property)]] [[File:Fujimori-jinja Auxiliary Hachiman-gu Shrine 001.jpg|thumb|Hachiman-gū building (Important Cultural Property)]] * '''Main Sanctuary''' (Kyoto City Tangible Cultural Property) **Built in Hōreki 5 (1755) as a temporary Naishi-dokoro (women’s quarters) within the Imperial Palace. Based on a surviving munafuda (ridge tag), it was likely moved here in Meiwa 4 (1767).{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000189691.html |title=京都市指定・登録文化財－建造物（伏見区） |access-date=2022-11-13 |publisher=京都市 文化市民局文化芸術都市推進室文化財保護課}} Originally granted by Emperor Nakamikado, it is said to be the oldest existing Naishi-dokoro. * '''{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Haisho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho}}''' (worship site) (Kyoto City Tangible Cultural Property) * '''Haiden''' (divided worship hall, 割拝殿) * '''Tenmangū-sha''' (shrine to Sugawara no Michizane) * '''Daishōgun-sha''' (Important Cultural Property, national) — According to tradition, built in Eikyō 10 (1438) under Ashikaga Yoshinori. * '''Shichinomiya-sha''' (Seven Shrines) — housing Zao Gongen, Hirota, Suwa, Itsukushima, Sumiyoshi, Kumano, and Tenman deities * '''Soreisha''' — Honors individuals who rendered significant service to the shrineInformation board on site, posted by the shrine office. * '''Hachiman-gū''' (Important Cultural Property, national) — Also said to have been built in Eikyō 10 (1438) under Ashikaga Yoshinori. * '''Second Hydrangea Garden''' * '''Shin Yoroi Statue''' * '''御旗塚 (Mikibazuka/Hata-zuka)''' — Said to be where Empress Jingū planted her war banner (tōki). Considered the original site of the shrine. The stump of an old yew (ichii) tree here is called “Ichi no Ki-san” and is believed to cure back pain; it is said that {{ill|Kondō Isami|en|Kondō Isami|qid=Q467334|lt=Kondō Isami|1=Kondō Isami}} worshipped here. * '''Fujinomori Seven Gods of Fortune''' * '''Fuji no Mizu (Wisteria Water)''' * '''Fujinomori Inari-sha''' * '''Kagura Hall''' * '''Shrine Office''' * '''Saikan''' (purification hall) * '''Sanshūden''' (social hall) * '''Treasure Hall''' * '''Former Worship Hall''' (now the Ema Hall) * '''First Hydrangea Garden''' * '''Mōko-zuka''' (“Mongol Mound”) — There were once seven mounds (the “Seven Mounds”), said to contain the buried heads and captured arms of Mongol invaders. File:Fujimori-jinja Main Sanctuary 002.jpg|Main Sanctuary File:Fujimori-jinja Auxiliary Tenman-gu Shrine 001.jpg|Tenmangū-sha File:Fujimori-jinja Auxiliary Shichisha-gu Shrine 001.jpg|Shichinomiya-sha File:Fujimori-jinja Auxiliary Sorei-sha Shrine 001.jpg|Soreisha File:Fujimori-jinja Hta Monument 001.jpg|Hata-zuka and the yew tree File:Fujimori-jinja Seven Lucky Gods 001.jpg|Seven Lucky Gods File:Fujimori-jinja Auxiliary Fujimori-Inari Shrine 001.jpg|Fujinomori Inari-sha File:Fujimori-jinja Mōko stone monument 001.jpg|Mōko-zuka == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * '''Hachiman-gū Main Hall of Fujinomori Shrine''' — Designated on April 14, 1906{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1922 |title=藤森神社境内社八幡宮本殿 ／ 国指定文化財等データベース |access-date=2022-11-13 |publisher=文化庁}}. * '''Daishōgun-sha Hall of Fujinomori Shrine''' — Designated on April 14, 1906{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1921 |title=藤森神社境内社大将軍社社殿 ／ 国指定文化財等データベース |access-date=2022-11-13 |publisher=文化庁}}. * '''Wooden Koma-inu (one pair)''' — Heian period; designated on May 6, 1936{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3516 |title=木造狛犬 ／ 国指定文化財等データベース |access-date=2022-11-13 |publisher=文化庁}}. Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|qid=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}.http://www.fujinomorijinjya.or.jp/oyasiro.html * '''Purple-Laced O-yoroi Armor with Ōsode''' (附: Akoda-type 28-plate helmet and remnants of Ō-tategi sune-ate) — Designated on March 20, 1954{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/5607 |title=紫絲威鎧 大袖付 ／ 国指定文化財等データベース |access-date=2022-11-13 |publisher=文化庁}}. === Kyoto City Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Five Buildings of Fujinomori Shrine''' — Designated June 1, 1984{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000189691.html |title=京都市指定・登録文化財－建造物（伏見区） |access-date=2022-11-13 |publisher=京都市　文化市民局文化芸術都市推進室文化財保護課}}: ** Main Sanctuary (附 two munafuda from Hōreki 5 and Meiwa 4) ** Heiden (offering hall) ** {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Haisho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho}} (worship site) ** East–West Corridors (2 structures) (附: tōrōbei fence, 1 structure) === Kyoto City Protected Cultural Property Environment === * '''Fujinomori Shrine Cultural Property Environment Preservation District''' — Covers the shrine precincts. Designated June 1, 1984.{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.kyoto.jp/kankyo/rdb/eco/sd/sd027.html |title=文化財環境保全地区 |access-date=2022-11-13 |publisher=京都府環境部自然環境保全課}} == Festivals == On {{ill|May 5|en|May 5|qid=Q2550|lt=May 5|1=May 5}}, the '''Fujimori Matsuri''' (alternately called the '''Fukakusa Matsuri''') is held. It is considered the origin of the samurai-themed Shōbu (iris) festival. The festival’s Kakeuma (horseback acrobatic) ritual is recorded in the 14th century as featuring warrior parades.「京都の神社と祭り」p134-135 * '''Shinkōsai (Portable Shrine Procession)''' — Three mikoshi from the East seat (Fukakusa-gō), West seat (Tōfukuji Kami-no-Gō), and central seat (Miyamoto Shimo-no-Gō) are carried through the shrine’s parish districts. * '''Warrior Parade''' — Divided into four segments: “Asawatari” (morning crossing, reminiscent of Prince Sawara’s army), “Konma” (imperial cavalry, referring to Emperor Seiwa), “Shichifukujin” (Seven Lucky Gods), and “Hotte” (God of War or Empress Jingū). They travel with the mikoshi across parishioner areas. * '''Kakeuma Shinji''' (horseback acrobatics) — Riders perform various stunts, such as Fuji-oriori, Tezuna-kuguri, Sakadachi, Yokonori, Ichiji-gaki, Yabarai, and Sakunori.『創祀千八百年　藤森神社』 During the mikoshi procession, the deity travels to the {{ill|Fujio-sha|ja|藤尾社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujio-sha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the grounds of {{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|en|Fushimi Inari-taisha|qid=Q714828|lt=Fushimi Inari Taisha|1=Fushimi Inari-taisha}}, which is said to be the shrine’s original site prior to relocation. == Access == * About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|JR Fujinomori Station|en|JR Fujinomori Station|qid=Q1048325|lt=JR Fujimori Station|1=JR Fujinomori Station}} on the JR Nara Line or from {{ill|Sumizome Station|en|Sumizome Station|qid=Q2153618|lt=Sumizome Station|1=Sumizome Station}} on the Keihan Main Line. == Notes == {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Fujinomori Shrine. (2007). 『創祀千八百年　藤森神社』 * Nishino Yuki & Suzuki Yasuhisa. (2011). 『京都鴨川探訪』Jinbun Shoin. * Heibonsha (ed.). (1997). 『京都・山城寺院神社大辞典』 * Honda Ken’ichi. (2015). 『京都の神社と祭り　千年都市における歴史と空間』Chūkō Shinsho 2345, ISBN 978-4-12-102345-2 == External Links == {{Commons|Category:Fujimori-jinja}} * [http://www.fujinomorijinjya.or.jp/index.html Fujinomori Shrine Official Website] {{citation needed|ja|藤森神社 (摂津市)|version=90473738|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[ja:藤森神社]] }} [[ja:%E8%97%A4%E6%A3%AE%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E6%91%82%E6%B4%A5%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:藤森神社 (京都市)]] {{translated page|ja|藤森神社|version=102175893|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Commons category|Fujimori-jinja}} [[zh:藤森神社]] {{Infobox religious building |名称={{ill|Fujinomori Shrine|ja|藤森神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinomori Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=File:Fujimori-jinja Main Sanctuary 003.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|location={{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|Settsu, Osaka|en|Settsu, Osaka|qid=Q643878|lt=Settsu, Osaka|1=Settsu, Osaka}} Torikai-nishi 2-chōme 1-1 |位置={{ill|coordinates (geographic)|ja|ウィキ座標2段度分秒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/coordinates (geographic)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 34°46′19.6″N 135°34′56.4″E | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|deity={{ill|Prince Toneri|en|Prince Toneri|qid=Q1362200|lt=Prince Toneri|1=Prince Toneri}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q382005}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|type={{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qid=Q712534|lt=Gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |創建= |本殿= | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|festival={{ill|October 18|en|October 18|qid=Q2964|lt=October 18|1=October 18}} |prefecture={{ill|Fushimi-ku|qid=Q1197771|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.951361|N|135.771694|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style={{ill|rake-overhanging gabled roof|qid=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} }} [[File:Fujimori settsu02.jpg|thumb|250px|Haiden]] '''Fujinomori Shrine''' (藤森神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Settsu, Osaka|en|Settsu, Osaka|qid=Q643878|lt=Settsu, Osaka|1=Settsu, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. == Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Prince Toneri|en|Prince Toneri|qid=Q1362200|lt=Prince Toneri|1=Prince Toneri}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q382005}} == History == In the {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zue|ja|摂津名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsu Meisho Zue|1=Settsu Meisho Zue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498549}}, it is written: “Located in Torikai-nishi village. It is the tutelary shrine for five villages in this area. The annual festival is on the Ninth Day of the Ninth Month. The enshrined deity, Emperor Sūdō Shinkyō, was transferred from the Fujimori Shrine in Yamashū.” Accordingly, {{ill|Prince Toneri|en|Prince Toneri|qid=Q1362200|lt=Prince Toneri|1=Prince Toneri}} was invited from {{ill|Fujinomori Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|藤森神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinomori Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} in Kyoto. Because the shrine is near the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|qid=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}}, it was often damaged by floods. On one occasion, the {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|qid=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}} was washed away downriver to {{ill|Suita, Osaka|ja|吹田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suita, Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吹田市|1=Suita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q653510}}, after which an {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|qid=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}} was established in the grounds of {{ill|Takahama Shrine|ja|高浜神社 (吹田市)|lt=Takahama Shrine (Suita)|lt_ja=高浜神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahama Shrine|qq=}}. The current sanctuary dates to the 5th year of the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qid=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} era (1577).『大阪府全志』{{全国書誌番号|73012615}} Scholar Tenbō Yukihiko noted that this region was originally inhabited by the {{ill|Torikai-be|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torikai-be|qq=}} clan, mentioned in both the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} and the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, whose members worshipped their ancestral deity Ame-no-Yugaketa-no-Mikoto. Later, because the shrine’s name matched that of Fujimori in Kyoto, its enshrined deity came to be regarded as {{ill|Prince Toneri|en|Prince Toneri|qid=Q1362200|lt=Prince Toneri|1=Prince Toneri}}.天坊幸彦「三島郡の史跡と名勝」（1961st year ）（大阪府学務部編『大阪府史蹟名勝天然記念物』 三島郡（1974th year ）に収録 {{全国書誌番号|73009804}}） * In the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} (1872), it was officially Ranked as a {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qid=Q712534|lt=Gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. * In Meiji 40 (1907) January, it was designated a shrine receiving offerings from the government (神饌幣帛料供進社). * On January 15, 1908 (Meiji 41), it absorbed Sanbonmatsu Tenjinsha of Torikai-nishi, Dōsojinsha of Torikai-naka, and Wakamiya Jinja of Torikai-kami. * In March 1910 (Meiji 43), it absorbed the {{ill|Murasha|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} Hachiman-gū in Shinzaike (one of three split-off enshrinements from Befu, Ichiya, and Shinzaike). * In January 1910 (Meiji 43), the Inari Shrines of Torikai Hatchō and Torikainono were merged into this shrine’s subsidiary Inari Shrine. == Subsidiary Shrines == * Ichikishima Jinja — {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵嶋姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} * Kuni no Satsuchi Jinja — {{ill|Kuni no Satsuchi no Mikoto|ja|国狭槌尊|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuni no Satsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * Inari Jinja — {{ill|Ukanomitama|en|Ukanomitama|qid=Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} == Points of Interest in the Vicinity == * Former site of Sanbonmatsu Tenjinsha ** {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q382005}}, exiled to Dazaifu, is said to have stopped his boat here at Torikai en route. After eating, he planted his own toothpick as a pine sprig, saying, “Should it sprout leaves, it will be an omen of my return to the capital.” Later, the pine did indeed grow, splitting into three trunks. Villagers built a shrine here to worship Michizane, which was later merged into Fujinomori Shrine in the Meiji era. * Site of Torikai Pasture (Torikai-no-Maki) ** The {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} mentions {{ill|Shokoku Maki|ja|近都牧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokoku Maki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 諸国牧}}, one of six ranches established around the capital, where cattle and horses were brought from various provinces, raised, and then sent to the court. * Torikai Ferry Crossing Site ** According to tradition, in {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 19 (1614), {{ill|Katagiri Katsumoto|en|Katagiri Katsumoto|qid=Q1882775|lt=Katagiri Katsumoto|1=Katagiri Katsumoto}} used this ferry when entering {{ill|Ibaraki Castle|ja|茨木城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Castle|1=Ibaraki Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11617856}}. From Shōwa 8 (1933) until Shōwa 50 (1975), the Osaka Prefectural “Torikai Ferry” operated at this location. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Kazumasa Moriyama|ja|森山一正|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazumasa Moriyama|1=Kazumasa Moriyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q57538671}} == External Links == * [http://www.fujimori.ed.jp/jinjya/index.html Torikai-chinza Fujinomori Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Osaka}} {{translated page|ja|藤森神社 (摂津市)|version=90473738|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:藤森神社]] [[ja:藤森神社 (摂津市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11624431}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Settsu, Osaka]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Kyoto]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:History of Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:History of Settsu, Osaka]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Kyoto City]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Takeshiuchi no Sukune]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
